<!-- GENERATED by claude-power-pack - scripts/codex-skill-sync.py; edit .claude/commands/documentation/c4.md instead -->

# C4 Architecture Diagram Generation

Analyze the current project and render its architecture as C4 diagrams using
Mermaid. Rendering is done by a zero-dependency Python engine
(`scripts/c4-mermaid.py`) that emits **GitHub-renderable** Mermaid:

- **L1-L3** -> Mermaid `flowchart` with `subgraph` boundaries and `classDef`
  C4 colours.
- **L4** -> Mermaid `classDiagram` (the code level is modelled as UML).

`flowchart` and `classDiagram` render inline on GitHub, mermaid.live, and VS
Code. (The Mermaid **C4 extension** - `C4Context`/`C4Container` - is *not*
bundled by GitHub and would show as raw text, so it is intentionally not used.)

## Arguments

- `LEVEL` (optional): Generate only a specific level - `L1`, `L2`, `L3`, or `L4`. Default: all 4 levels.

## Instructions

### Step 1: Analyze the Project

Read the project's key files to understand its architecture:

1. **CLAUDE.md** - Project overview, components, structure
2. **README.md** - High-level description
3. **Directory structure** - `ls` the top-level directories
4. **Key config files** - `pyproject.toml`, `package.json`, `docker-compose.yml`, etc.
5. **Existing docs** - Check `docs/architecture/` for previous C4 outputs

Build a model of:
- Who uses the system (actors/personas)
- What external systems it integrates with
- What containers (apps, DBs, services) make up the system
- What components exist within each container
- What key code elements (classes, modules) implement each component

**C4 Node Types** (set each node's `type` to one of these):

| Node Type | C4 Concept | When to Use | Colour |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| `person` | Actor / User | External users, admins, operators | Dark blue (circle) |
| `system` | External System | Third-party APIs, services not owned by the team | Grey |
| `system-focus` | System of Interest | The system being documented | Blue |
| `container` | Container | Applications, databases, message queues, file stores | Green |
| `component` | Component | Modules, services, controllers within a container | Purple |
| `code` | Code Element | Classes, interfaces, functions, data models | Amber |

### Step 2: Build the C4 Model (JSON)

Write the model to `docs/architecture/c4-model.json`. This is the single source
of truth the renderer consumes; keep it version-controlled.

```bash
DOCS_DIR="docs/architecture"
mkdir -p "$DOCS_DIR"
```

Model schema:

```json
{
  "project": "My Project",
  "levels": [
    {
      "id": "c4-l1-context",
      "level": "L1",
      "title": "L1 System Context",
      "kind": "flowchart",
      "parent": null,
      "nodes": [
        {"id": "user", "label": "User", "type": "person"},
        {"id": "api", "label": "API Server", "type": "container"},
        {"id": "db", "label": "PostgreSQL", "type": "container", "shape": "cylinder"},
        {"id": "ext", "label": "Third-party API", "type": "system"}
      ],
      "edges": [
        {"source": "user", "target": "api", "label": "Uses"},
        {"source": "api", "target": "db"},
        {"source": "api", "target": "ext", "label": "Calls"}
      ],
      "boundaries": [
        {"id": "sys", "label": "System of Interest", "nodes": ["api", "db"]}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Rules for building the model:

- **L1 System Context** (`kind: flowchart`): actors + external systems + the
  system of interest (5-10 nodes). Group the owned system's pieces with a
  `boundaries` entry.
- **L2 Container** (`kind: flowchart`): applications, data stores, services
  within the system boundary; note the technology in the label.
- **L3 Component** (`kind: flowchart`): **one level object per container**,
  `id: c4-l3-<container-slug>`, `parent: c4-l2-container`. 5-15 components each;
  include edges to components in other containers.
- **L4 Code** (`kind: classdiagram`): top 3 most architecturally significant
  components per container. Use `classes` + `relations` instead of
  `nodes`/`edges`:
  ```json
  {
    "id": "c4-l4-api-auth", "level": "L4", "kind": "classdiagram",
    "title": "L4 Code - Auth (API Server)", "parent": "c4-l3-api",
    "classes": [
      {"name": "AuthController", "members": ["+login()", "-token: str"]},
      {"name": "TokenStore"}
    ],
    "relations": [
      {"source": "AuthController", "target": "TokenStore", "kind": "-->", "label": "uses"}
    ]
  }
  ```

Node `shape` (optional): `rect` (default), `round`, `circle` (default for
`person`), `cylinder` (data stores), `hexagon`.

Keep node `id`s unique **across the whole model** by prefixing with the
container slug (e.g. `api-authctl`) so L3/L4 references never collide.

If `LEVEL` was supplied, include only that level's objects in `levels`.

### Step 3: Render with the Mermaid engine

Run the renderer (pass an ISO timestamp so the manifest/index are reproducible):

```bash
python3 scripts/c4-mermaid.py \
  --model "$DOCS_DIR/c4-model.json" \
  --out "$DOCS_DIR" \
  --project "<Project Name>" \
  --timestamp "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
```

> The renderer lives in your claude-power-pack checkout. When running inside a
> different project, invoke it by its absolute path
> (`python3 /path/to/claude-power-pack/scripts/c4-mermaid.py ...`).

The engine writes, into `docs/architecture/`:
- `c4-<level>.mmd` - one Mermaid file per level (the `.mmd` extension renders in
  VS Code / mermaid.live; the content is plain text and diffable).
- `index.md` - embeds every diagram in a ```mermaid fence, so the whole model
  renders inline on GitHub.
- `c4-manifest.json` - parent/child index of all diagrams for downstream tools.

### Step 4: QA gating (edge validity + density)

The renderer validates every level and prints warnings:

- **`edge_validity` / `relation_validity` / `duplicate_*` (high severity):**
  an edge/relation references an undefined node, or an id is duplicated. The
  command **exits non-zero**. Fix the offending id in `c4-model.json` and re-run
  Step 3. (Do NOT pass `--lenient` to paper over these.)
- **`density` (medium):** a diagram exceeds 20 nodes. Split it - for L3, break
  the container into sub-boundaries or promote a cluster to its own L3 object -
  then re-run.
- **`boundary_validity` (medium) / `orphan_node` (low):** note in the report;
  fix boundaries; orphans are usually intentional top-level actors.

Track the warning counts for the final report.

### Step 5: Review CLAUDE.md and README.md

After generating diagrams, review these files for accuracy:

1. **CLAUDE.md** - Check the repository-structure section matches the current
   layout. Flag stale references.
2. **README.md** - Check the project description matches the architecture shown.

Report any issues found but do NOT auto-edit these files. Ask the user for
confirmation before making changes.

### Step 6: Report

```
C4 Architecture Diagrams Generated

  Output: docs/architecture/

  L1 Context:    c4-l1-context.mmd       ({n} nodes, {e} edges)
  L2 Container:  c4-l2-container.mmd      ({n} nodes, {e} edges)
  L3 Component:  {N} containers -> c4-l3-*.mmd
  L4 Code:       {M} components -> c4-l4-*.mmd  (classDiagram)

  index.md         (renders all diagrams inline on GitHub)
  c4-manifest.json ({2 + N + M} diagrams tracked)

  QA:
    Invalid references (high): {count}  (must be 0)
    Density splits suggested:  {count}
    Orphan / boundary notes:   {count}

  Doc Review:
    CLAUDE.md:  {OK | {N} issues found}
    README.md:  {OK | {N} issues found}

  View: open index.md on GitHub, or any .mmd in VS Code / mermaid.live.
```

## Notes

- The C4 model was created by Simon Brown - see https://c4model.com
- L1 and L2 are single diagrams; L3 is one per container; L4 is up to 3 per container.
- The `.mmd` files and `index.md` are plain text - commit them; they are the
  version-controllable source of truth (no binary image artifacts required).
- Regenerate after major architectural changes.
- Use `/documentation-pptx` to build a presentation; supply pre-rendered diagram
  images (export a `.mmd` from mermaid.live) if you want raster slides.
